content.problemStatement:
Sergey recently learned about **country codes** - two letter strings, denoting countries. For example, **BY** stands for Belarus and **IN** stands for India. Mesmerized by this new discovery, Sergey now looks for country codes everywhere!

Sergey has recently found a string **S** consisting of uppercase Latin letters. He wants to find the number of different country codes that appear in **S** as contiguous substrings. For the purpose of this problem, consider that every **2**-letter uppercase string is a valid country code.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a non‑empty string S consisting only of uppercase Latin letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with one integer – the number of distinct 2‑letter substrings (country codes) that appear in S.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: substrings are IN, NB, BY → 3 distinct codes. Test case 2: substrings are BY, YB, BY → distinct codes BY and YB → 2.
Substrings are AB and BC, both different.
All substrings are AA, so only one distinct code.

solution.approach:
For each test case we slide a window of length 2 over the string. Every window produces a 2‑letter code. There are only 26·26 = 676 possible codes, so we can map each code to an integer index: (first‑letter-'A')*26 + (second‑letter-'A'). A boolean array of size 676 records whether a code has already been seen. While scanning the string we mark unseen codes and increase a counter. After the scan the counter equals the number of distinct codes.

The algorithm runs in O(|S|) time per test case and uses O(676) = O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(|S|) per test case, overall O( Σ|S| ) for the whole input.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – a fixed array of 676 booleans (or a set of at most 676 elements).
